    Documentation

    Getting Started

    Install LeanCTX and start saving tokens in under 5 minutes. Step-by-step guide for macOS, Linux, and Windows.

    On this page

    Installation

    Overview Install Binary Setup Verify

    Advanced

    Monitor Savings Multi-Agent (lctx) Updating Uninstalling Troubleshooting

    Other Docs

    CLI Reference Analytics & Dashboards Configuration Quick Reference Tool API Reference

    Installation

    • Overview
    • Install Binary
    • Setup
    • Verify

    Advanced

    • Monitor Savings
    • Multi-Agent (lctx)
    • Updating
    • Uninstalling
    • Troubleshooting

    Other Docs

    • CLI Reference
    • Analytics & Dashboards
    • Configuration
    • Quick Reference
    • Tool API Reference

    LeanCTX reduces your LLM's token consumption by up to 99% through a single Rust binary that provides: a Shell Hook with 90+ CLI compression patterns, an MCP Server with 24 tools for cached reads and session intelligence, and a Persistent Project Graph for structural code understanding. Install it once, and every file read, shell command, and code navigation becomes more efficient.

    Prompt Generator

    Select your platform and AI tool below. The generated prompt can be pasted into any AI coding assistant — it will walk through the full installation and configuration automatically.

    generated prompt 
     
     
     
       
    Get Started in 3 Steps
     
     
     
     
     
     3 commands — that's it 
     
     
    # 1. Install (pick one)
curl -fsSL https://leanctx.com/install.sh | sh          # universal
brew tap yvgude/lean-ctx && brew install lean-ctx        # macOS / Linux
npm install -g lean-ctx-bin                              # Node.js
cargo install lean-ctx                                   # Rust

# 2. Setup (auto-configures shell + ALL detected editors)
lean-ctx setup

# 3. Restart your shell
source ~/.zshrc  # or ~/.bashrc
     
     
     
     lean-ctx setup automatically detects and configures:
Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf,
VS Code / Copilot, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI,
Zed, Antigravity, OpenCode, and Pi.
    Run lean-ctx doctor to verify everything works.

     
     
Detailed installation instructions per platform
 
    Step 1: Install the Binary
     
    Universal Installer (any platform)
     
    The fastest way to install lean-ctx without any prerequisites:
     
    curl -fsSL https://leanctx.com/install.sh | sh
     
    This auto-detects your OS and architecture, downloads the correct pre-built binary from GitHub Releases, verifies the SHA256 checksum, and installs to /usr/local/bin/. No Rust toolchain required.
     
    For manual control, download the script first:
     
    curl -fsSL https://leanctx.com/install.sh -o install.sh
chmod +x install.sh
./install.sh --download    # pre-built binary (no Rust)
./install.sh               # build from source (requires Rust)
     
    npm (Node.js)
     
    Install the pre-built binary via npm — no Rust toolchain required:
     
    npm install -g lean-ctx-bin
     
    This downloads the correct binary for your platform during postinstall with SHA256 verification. Works on macOS (Intel + Apple Silicon), Linux (x86_64), and Windows (x86_64).
     
    macOS
     
    Option A: Homebrew (recommended)
     
    brew tap yvgude/lean-ctx
brew install lean-ctx
     
    This installs the lean-ctx binary to /opt/homebrew/bin/ (Apple Silicon) or /usr/local/bin/ (Intel). Both are already in your PATH.
     
    Option B: Cargo (requires Rust)
     
    cargo install lean-ctx
     
    Installs to ~/.cargo/bin/lean-ctx. Make sure ~/.cargo/bin is in your PATH:
     
    echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.cargo/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.zshrc
source ~/.zshrc
     
    Option C: Pre-built binary
     
       
    1. Download from GitHub Releases:

         
      • Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4): lean-ctx-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.gz
        •  
      • Intel Mac: lean-ctx-x86_64-apple-darwin.tar.gz
        •  
       
      2.  
    2. Extract and move to PATH:

      tar xzf lean-ctx-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.gz
chmod +x lean-ctx
sudo mv lean-ctx /usr/local/bin/
       
      3.  
     
    macOS Gatekeeper note: If you see "cannot be opened because the developer cannot be verified", run:
     
    xattr -d com.apple.quarantine /usr/local/bin/lean-ctx
     
    Linux
     
    Option A: Homebrew
     
    brew tap yvgude/lean-ctx && brew install lean-ctx
     
    Option B: Arch Linux (AUR)
     
    # Source build from crates.io
yay -S lean-ctx

# Or pre-built binary from GitHub
yay -S lean-ctx-bin
     
    Works with any AUR helper: yay, paru, pikaur, trizen.
     
    Option C: Cargo
     
    cargo install lean-ctx
     
    Requires the Rust toolchain. Install it with curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh.
     
    Option D: Pre-built binary
     
       
    1. Download lean-ctx-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.tar.gz from GitHub Releases
      2.  
    2. Extract and install:

      tar xzf lean-ctx-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.tar.gz
chmod +x lean-ctx
sudo mv lean-ctx /usr/local/bin/
       
      3.  
     
    Windows
     
    Option A: Pre-built binary (recommended — no Rust needed)
     
       
    1. Go to github.com/yvgude/lean-ctx/releases/latest
      2.  
    2. Download lean-ctx-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip
      3.  
    3. Extract the ZIP — you'll get lean-ctx.exe
      4.  
    4. Move lean-ctx.exe to a folder in your PATH, for example:

      mkdir %USERPROFILE%\bin
move lean-ctx.exe %USERPROFILE%\bin\
       
      5.  
    5. Add that folder to your PATH:

         
      • Open Settings → System → About → Advanced system settings → Environment Variables
        •  
      • Under "User variables", select Path, click Edit, then New
        •  
      • Add %USERPROFILE%\bin
        •  
      • Click OK on all dialogs
        •  
       
      6.  
    6. Open a new PowerShell/CMD window and verify:

      lean-ctx --version
       
      7.  
     
    Option B: Cargo
     
       
    1. Install the Rust toolchain (download and run rustup-init.exe)
      2.  
    2. Open a new PowerShell window and run:

      cargo install lean-ctx
       
      3.  
    3. The binary is installed to %USERPROFILE%\.cargo\bin\lean-ctx.exe which rustup adds to your PATH automatically.
      4.  
     
    Option C: Build from source
     
    git clone https://github.com/yvgude/lean-ctx.git
cd lean-ctx\rust
cargo build --release
copy target\release\lean-ctx.exe %USERPROFILE%\bin\
     
    Windows + WSL: If you use Windows Subsystem for Linux, follow the Linux instructions inside your WSL distribution. The shell hook and MCP server work identically in WSL.
     
    Build from Source (any platform)
     
    git clone https://github.com/yvgude/lean-ctx.git
cd lean-ctx/rust
cargo build --release
     
    The binary is at target/release/lean-ctx (or lean-ctx.exe on Windows). Copy it to a directory in your PATH.
     
     
    Step 2: Setup
     
    After installing the binary, run lean-ctx setup to configure everything automatically:
     
    lean-ctx setup
     
    This single command:
     
       
    • Installs 23 shell aliases (git, npm, cargo, docker, kubectl, curl, and more)
      •  
    • Auto-detects installed editors (Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf, VS Code/Copilot, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, Zed, Antigravity, OpenCode)
      •  
    • Creates MCP config files for each detected editor
      •  
    • Installs agent instruction files
      •  
    • Runs lean-ctx doctor to verify everything
      •  
     
    After setup, restart your shell:
     
      
    ShellRestart command
           
    Zsh (macOS default)source ~/.zshrc
     
    Bashsource ~/.bashrc
     
    Fishsource ~/.config/fish/config.fish
     
    PowerShellRestart PowerShell
          
     
    Then restart your editor completely.
     
    Manual alternative
     
    If you prefer to install only shell aliases without editor auto-detection:
     
    lean-ctx init --global
     
    Then configure your editor manually using the instructions in Step 3 below.
     
    Step 3: Verify
     
       
    1. Check the binary:

      lean-ctx --version   # → lean-ctx 2.7.0
lean-ctx doctor      # checks PATH, config, aliases, MCP
       
      2.  
    2. Test the shell hook:

      git status           # output is now compressed
lean-ctx gain        # shows token savings so far
       
      3.  
    3. In your editor, verify that 24 MCP tools appear (e.g. ctx_read, ctx_shell, ctx_metrics).
      4.  
     
     
Manual editor configuration (only if lean-ctx setup didn't work)
 
     If you used lean-ctx setup, your editor is likely already configured.
    Run lean-ctx doctor to check. If it shows a green check for your editor, skip this section.

     
    
Otherwise, connect lean-ctx as an MCP server manually. Each editor has its own config format.

     
     
    Before you start: Find your lean-ctx binary path. You'll need it if the short name "lean-ctx" doesn't work.
     
    # macOS / Linux:
which lean-ctx
# Example output: /opt/homebrew/bin/lean-ctx  or  /Users/you/.cargo/bin/lean-ctx

# Windows (PowerShell):
where.exe lean-ctx
# Example output: C:\Users\you\.cargo\bin\lean-ctx.exe
     
    Keep this path handy — some editors need the full path instead of just "lean-ctx".
     
     
    Cursor
     
       
    1. Open or create ~/.cursor/mcp.json:

      # macOS / Linux:
mkdir -p ~/.cursor
nano ~/.cursor/mcp.json

# Windows (PowerShell):
mkdir -Force "$env:USERPROFILE\.cursor"
notepad "$env:USERPROFILE\.cursor\mcp.json"
       
      2.  
    2. Paste this exact content (the entire file):

      {
  "mcpServers": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "command": "lean-ctx"
    }
  }
}
       
      3.  
    3. Restart Cursor completely (Cmd+Q / Ctrl+Q, then reopen)
      4.  
    4. Verify: Open the MCP panel — you should see 24 tools listed under "lean-ctx"
      5.  
     
    Optionally, install the Cursor rule for maximum savings:
     
    lean-ctx init --agent cursor
     
    Claude Code
     
    Claude Code has a built-in CLI command for adding MCP servers — no manual file editing needed:
     
    # Add lean-ctx as MCP server:
claude mcp add lean-ctx lean-ctx

# Install the CLAUDE.md instructions (makes Claude use lean-ctx tools):
lean-ctx init --agent claude
     
    That's it. Restart Claude Code and the 24 tools will be available.
     
    GitHub Copilot (VS Code)
     
       
    1. In your project root, create .github/copilot/mcp.json:

      mkdir -p .github/copilot
       
      2.  
    2. Create the file with this content:

      {
  "servers": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "command": "lean-ctx"
    }
  }
}
       
      3.  
    3. Restart VS Code
      4.  
     
     VS Code native MCP: Running lean-ctx init --agent copilot creates .vscode/mcp.json in your project so VS Code's built-in MCP integration can use lean-ctx alongside (or instead of) the Copilot-specific file above.

     
    Note: This is per-project. To use lean-ctx across all projects, repeat for each project or add it to your global VS Code settings.
     
    Windsurf
     
       
    1. Open or create the config file:

      # macOS:
mkdir -p ~/.codeium/windsurf
nano ~/.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json

# Linux:
mkdir -p ~/.codeium/windsurf
nano ~/.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json

# Windows:
mkdir -Force "$env:USERPROFILE\.codeium\windsurf"
notepad "$env:USERPROFILE\.codeium\windsurf\mcp_config.json"
       
      2.  
    2. Paste this exact content:

      {
  "mcpServers": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "command": "/FULL/PATH/TO/lean-ctx"
    }
  }
}
       
       Important — use the FULL PATH! Replace /FULL/PATH/TO/lean-ctx with the output
        of which lean-ctx. Windsurf spawns MCP servers with a minimal PATH that often doesn't include
~/.cargo/bin or /opt/homebrew/bin. Example:

       
      {
  "mcpServers": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "command": "/opt/homebrew/bin/lean-ctx"
    }
  }
}
       
      3.  
    3. Restart Windsurf completely
      4.  
    4. Open the MCP panel and verify 24 tools appear
      5.  
     
    Zed
     
       
    1. Open Zed settings (Cmd+, on macOS, or Cmd+Shift+P → "zed: open settings"):

      # macOS / Linux:
~/.config/zed/settings.json

# Windows:
%APPDATA%\Zed\settings.json
       
      2.  
    2. Add the context_servers section (merge with any existing settings):

      {
  "context_servers": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "source": "custom",
      "command": "lean-ctx",
      "args": [],
      "env": {}
    }
  }
}
       
       Important: The "source": "custom" field is required — without it, Zed silently ignores the server.
        If "lean-ctx" doesn't work, use the full path (output of which lean-ctx).

       
      3.  
    3. Zed auto-restarts the MCP server after saving settings (no editor restart needed)
      4.  
    4. Verify: Open the Agent Panel (Cmd+Shift+A) → click the settings icon (top-right gear) → look for a green dot next to "lean-ctx"
      5.  
     
     Tip: To auto-approve lean-ctx tool calls, add "agent": {"always_allow_tool_actions": true} to your settings.
    For per-tool control, use "tool_permissions": {"mcp:lean-ctx:ctx_read": "allow"}.

     
    OpenAI Codex CLI
     
       
    1. Open or create ~/.codex/config.toml:

      # macOS / Linux:
mkdir -p ~/.codex
nano ~/.codex/config.toml

# Windows:
mkdir -Force "$env:USERPROFILE\.codex"
notepad "$env:USERPROFILE\.codex\config.toml"
       
      2.  
    2. Add this section (keep any existing content):

      [mcp_servers.lean-ctx]
command = "lean-ctx"
args = []
       
      3.  
    3. Restart Codex CLI
      4.  
     
    Alternative — use the Codex CLI command instead of editing the file:
     
    codex mcp add lean-ctx
     
    Then install the agent instructions:
     
    lean-ctx init --agent codex
     
    Google Gemini CLI / Antigravity
     
       
    1. Create the config directory and file:

      # macOS / Linux:
mkdir -p ~/.gemini/settings
nano ~/.gemini/settings/mcp.json

# Windows (PowerShell):
mkdir -Force "$env:USERPROFILE\.gemini\settings"
notepad "$env:USERPROFILE\.gemini\settings\mcp.json"
       
      2.  
    2. Paste this exact content (the entire file):

      {
  "mcpServers": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "command": "lean-ctx"
    }
  }
}
       
      If "lean-ctx" doesn't work, use the full path (output of which lean-ctx):
       
      {
  "mcpServers": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "command": "/opt/homebrew/bin/lean-ctx"
    }
  }
}
       
      3.  
    3. Restart the Gemini CLI session
      4.  
    4. Verify by asking Gemini to list available tools — you should see 24 lean-ctx tools
      5.  
     
    Common issue: If the file path is wrong, Gemini CLI silently ignores it. Make sure the directory exists and the JSON is valid (no trailing commas).
     
    Google Antigravity IDE
     
    Antigravity is Google's AI-first IDE with deep Gemini integration. It uses the standard mcpServers format.
     
       
    1. Open or create the config file:

      # macOS / Linux:
mkdir -p ~/.gemini/antigravity
nano ~/.gemini/antigravity/mcp_config.json

# Windows (PowerShell):
mkdir -Force "$env:USERPROFILE\.gemini\antigravity"
notepad "$env:USERPROFILE\.gemini\antigravity\mcp_config.json"
       
      2.  
    2. Paste this exact content:

      {
  "mcpServers": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "command": "lean-ctx"
    }
  }
}
       
      If "lean-ctx" doesn't work, use the full path (output of which lean-ctx).
       
      3.  
    3. Restart Antigravity
      4.  
    4. Verify: The 24 lean-ctx MCP tools should appear in the tool panel
      5.  
     
    You can also manage MCP servers through Antigravity's built-in MCP Store ("..." dropdown → "Manage MCP Servers" → "View raw config").
     
    OpenCode
     
       
    1. Open or create ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json (global) or opencode.json in your project root:

      # macOS / Linux:
mkdir -p ~/.config/opencode
nano ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json
       
      2.  
    2. Add the MCP section:

      {
  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",
  "mcp": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "type": "local",
      "command": ["lean-ctx"],
      "enabled": true
    }
  }
}
       
      3.  
    3. Restart OpenCode
      4.  
     
    OpenClaw
     
    OpenClaw uses skills instead of MCP. The shell hook gives you automatic compression:
     
       
    1. lean-ctx init --global — installs shell aliases
      2.  
    2. Restart your shell
      3.  
    3. Every exec call in OpenClaw is now automatically compressed
      4.  
     
    For deeper integration, install the LeanCTX skill into ~/.openclaw/skills/lean-ctx/.
     
    Pi Coding Agent
     
    Pi uses an extension-based system. The pi-lean-ctx package overrides Pi's built-in bash, read, grep, find, and ls tools to route them through lean-ctx.
     
       
    1. Install lean-ctx (if not already installed):

      cargo install lean-ctx
       
      2.  
    2. Install the Pi extension:

      pi install pi-lean-ctx
       
      Or use the lean-ctx installer:
       
      lean-ctx init --agent pi
       
      3.  
    3. All tools are now automatically routed through lean-ctx — no manual prefixing needed
      4.  
    4. Verify with the /lean-ctx slash command inside Pi
      5.  
     
     Smart reads: The extension automatically selects the optimal read mode based on file type and size
    (full for small files, map for medium code files, signatures for large code files).

     
    Other Editors with MCP Support
     
    Any editor that supports the Model Context Protocol can use lean-ctx. The general pattern:
     
    {
  "mcpServers": {
    "lean-ctx": {
      "command": "lean-ctx"
    }
  }
}
     
    Key points:
     
       
    • The command must point to the lean-ctx binary (use full path if the short name doesn't work)
      •  
    • No args needed — lean-ctx auto-detects MCP mode when invoked without arguments
      •  
    • No env or port config needed — lean-ctx uses stdio transport
      •  
    • Always restart the editor after changing MCP config
      •  
     
     
    Monitor Savings
     
    # Terminal dashboard (colors, bars, sparklines)
lean-ctx gain

# Web dashboard with charts
lean-ctx dashboard

# Find uncompressed commands in shell history
lean-ctx discover

# Run a real benchmark on your project
lean-ctx benchmark run
     
    Multi-Agent Launcher (lctx)
     
    
The lctx command auto-detects your AI agent, builds the project graph, and launches with lean-ctx pre-configured:

     
    # Auto-detect and launch
lctx

# Launch a specific agent
lctx cursor
lctx claude
lctx codex
lctx gemini

# List supported agents
lctx --list
     
    Supported agents: Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Google Gemini, Windsurf, Cline. The launcher:
     
       
    • Finds your project root (nearest .git directory)
      •  
    • Builds or updates the persistent project graph (ctx_graph build)
      •  
    • Launches the agent with lean-ctx MCP server configured
      •  
     
    Install the launcher script:
     
    # Copy to your PATH
sudo cp bin/lctx /usr/local/bin/
# Or symlink
sudo ln -sf $(pwd)/bin/lctx /usr/local/bin/lctx
     
    Updating
     
    The fastest way to update is the built-in self-update:
     
    lean-ctx update
     
    This downloads the latest release from GitHub, verifies the binary, and replaces the current installation. Works on all platforms.
     
    Alternatively, use the same method you used to install:
     
      
    MethodCommand
           
    Homebrewbrew update && brew upgrade lean-ctx
     
    Cargocargo install lean-ctx
     
    npmnpm update -g lean-ctx-bin
     
    AURyay -Syu lean-ctx
     
    install.shRe-run curl -fsSL https://leanctx.com/install.sh | sh
     
    BinaryDownload latest from GitHub Releases
          
     
    After updating, always re-run:
     
    lean-ctx setup           # re-configures shell + editors
source ~/.zshrc          # restart shell
     
    Uninstalling
     
    
Starting with v2.8.0, lean-ctx includes a built-in uninstall command that removes all configuration:

     
    lean-ctx uninstall
     
    This command removes:
     
       
    • Shell hook — removes the lean-ctx block from ~/.zshrc, ~/.bashrc, ~/.config/fish/config.fish, or your PowerShell profile
      •  
    • MCP configurations — removes the lean-ctx entry from Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf, Gemini CLI, Antigravity, Codex CLI, VS Code, and OpenCode
      •  
    • Data directory — removes ~/.lean-ctx/ (stats, sessions, cache, config)
      •  
     
    After running the command, remove the binary itself:
     
     
    Installed viaRemove command
      
    Cargocargo uninstall lean-ctx
     
    Homebrewbrew uninstall lean-ctx
     
    AURyay -R lean-ctx
     
    Manual binaryrm $(which lean-ctx)
          
     
    Then restart your terminal. All lean-ctx compression aliases and MCP tools will be gone.
     
    Troubleshooting
     
    lean-ctx: command not found
     
    The binary isn't in your PATH. Check where it was installed:
     
    # Cargo installs here:
ls ~/.cargo/bin/lean-ctx

# Homebrew installs here (macOS):
ls /opt/homebrew/bin/lean-ctx

# Manual installs — wherever you placed it:
ls /usr/local/bin/lean-ctx
     
    Add the correct directory to your PATH in your shell profile.
     
    MCP tools don't appear in editor
     
       
    • Make sure you restarted the editor completely after adding the MCP config
      •  
    • Check that lean-ctx --version works from the terminal the editor uses
      •  
    • Try using the full path to the binary in the MCP config (e.g. /Users/you/.cargo/bin/lean-ctx)
      •  
    • Run lean-ctx doctor to diagnose PATH and configuration issues
      •  
     
    Shell commands hang or recurse
     
    If cargo test or other commands hang after installing lean-ctx aliases, prefix with LEAN_CTX_ACTIVE=1 to bypass the alias:
     
    LEAN_CTX_ACTIVE=1 cargo test
     
    This is only needed when lean-ctx itself runs the same command internally (e.g. during builds or tests).
     
    macOS: "cannot be opened because the developer cannot be verified"
     
    xattr -d com.apple.quarantine $(which lean-ctx)
     
    Windows: lean-ctx.exe not recognized
     
    Make sure the folder containing lean-ctx.exe is in your PATH. Open a new terminal after changing PATH — existing terminals don't pick up changes.
     
    Next Steps
     
       
    • Project Graph — persistent dependency scanning with symbol lookup and impact analysis
      •  
    • Cognitive Efficiency Protocol (CEP) — how lean-ctx optimizes LLM reasoning
      •  
    • Context Continuity Protocol (CCP) — cross-session memory
      •  
    • Token Dense Dialect (TDD) — symbol shorthand for 8-25% more savings
      •  
    • All 24 MCP Tools
      •  
    • CLI & Configuration
      •

The Intelligence Layer for AI Coding.
